titleStmt.title: SII 3.56: original edition by Eugen Julius Theodor Hultzsch
V.‚ÄĒINSCRIPTIONS AT TIRUVALLAM. No. 56.‚ÄĒON THE SOUTH WALL OF THE MAHAMANDAPA IN THE BILVANATHESVARA TEMPLE.

summary.p: This inscription is incomplete. Of the five lines which are preserved I am publishing only the two first ones. It is dated in the 2nd year of the reign of RńĀjakńďsarivarman, alias RńĀjamahńďndradńďva, and records that a military officer purchased 800 kuŠłĽi (l. 4) of land from the inhabitants of Tiruvallam and granted them to the temple.

On page 32 above it has been stated that the KaliŠĻÖgattu-ParaŠĻái and Vikkirama-ŇöŇćŠłĽaŠĻČ-UlńĀ mention two ChŇćŠł∑a kings who have not yet been identified. The first of them reigned between RńĀjńďndra and VńęrarńĀjńďndra I., and the second between VńęrarńĀjńďndra I. and KulŇćttuŠĻÖga-ChŇćŠł∑a I. In the introductory remarks to No. 57 it will be shown that the second king is identical with Parakńďsarivarman, alias AdhirńĀjńďndradńďva. Hence the only king who remains to be identified is the successor of RńĀjńďndra and predecessor of VńęrarńĀjńďndra I. He may be identified provisionally with RńĀjakńďsarivarman, alias RńĀjamahńďndradńďva, to whose 2nd year the subjoined inscription belongs. In favour of this identification it may be mentioned that the subjoined inscription praises him for guiding the goddess of the earth on the path of Manu, while the KaliŠĻÖgattu-ParaŠĻái (viii. 28) speaks of ‚Äúthe ChŇćŠł∑a who dispensed justice three or four times better than the ancient Manu,‚ÄĚInd. Ant. Vol. XIX. p. 331. and that an inscription of the 9th year of RńĀjńďndra mentions among the boundaries of a village ‚Äúthe road of RńĀjamahńďndra.‚ÄĚSee p. 41 above. Perhaps RńĀjamahńďndra was the co-regent of RńĀjńďndra.

revisionDesc.change: Further conversion of digital encoding to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)
Conversion of digital encoding to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)

div.p:
svasti Ňõrńę . tirumakaŠł∑ viŠł∑aŠĻÖkavirunilamaŠĻ≠antaiyai OrukuŠĻ≠ai niŠłĽaŠĻükiŠłĽińęnŠĻČitiu niŠĻüpa puŠĻáarn=tu tarumaneŠĻüi niŠĻüppa maŠĻČuneŠĻüi ŠĻČaŠĻ≠ńĀttiya kovirńĀjakecarivanmarńĀna UŠĻ≠aiyńĀr ŇõrńęrńĀjamahentradevarkku yńĀŠĻáŠĻ≠u 2 ńÄvatu jayaŠĻÖkoŠĻáŠĻ≠acoŠłĽamaŠĻáŠĻ≠alattupperumpńĀŠĻáappńĀŠĻ≠ikkaraivaŠłĽittiruvallattu sabhaiyom nilavilaiyńĀvaŠĻáakkaiyyeŠłĽuttu . ArumoŠłĽitevavaŠł∑anńĀŠĻ≠ŠĻ≠u IŠĻÖkaŠĻáńĀŠĻ≠ŠĻ≠u IŠĻÖkaŠĻáŇęruŠĻ≠aiyńĀn caŠĻÖkaraŠĻČ kaŠĻáŠĻ≠a
rńĀtittanńĀrńĀŠĻČa senńĀpatikaŠł∑ rńĀjarńĀjaccoŠłĽiyavaraiyar pakkal nńĀŠĻÖkaŠł∑ koŠĻáŠĻ≠a AŠĻČŠĻüńĀŠĻ≠u naŠĻükńĀcu AŠĻüupattu nńĀlinńĀŠĻükńĀconŠĻüukkukkuŠĻ≠inaŠĻükal niŠĻüai cemmai pon EŠłĽuma√ĪcńĀŠĻ≠iyńĀka van=ta pon IrupattirukaŠłĽa√Īce EŠĻ≠ŠĻ≠u ma√ĪcńĀŠĻ≠iyum EŠĻÖkaŠł∑ŇęrttiruvallamuŠĻ≠aiya mahńĀdevarńĀtidńĀsan caŠĻáŠłćeŇõvaradevar pak=kal Ippon IrupattirukaŠłĽa√Īce EŠĻ≠ŠĻ≠u ma√ĪcńĀŠĻ≠iyuŠĻÖkoŠĻáŠĻ≠u nńĀŠĻÖkaŠł∑ viŠĻükiŠĻüa bhŇęmi rńĀjen=tira
coŠłĽappereri kińęŠłĽ koŠĻ≠acceŠĻüuvukkukkińęŠłĽpńĀŠĻükellai

(Line 1.) Hail ! Prosperity ! In the 2nd year (of the reign) of king RńĀjakńďsarivarman, alias the lord Ňörńę-RńĀjamahńďndradńďva, who, while the goddess of fortune was resplendent, wedded the great goddess of the earth, in order that (she) might abide joyfully under the shade of a single parasol, and who caused (her) to walk (in) the path of Manu, in order that (she) might abide (in) the way of righteousness. The hand-writing, (referring to) a deed of sale of land, of us, the assembly of Tiruvallam in KaraivaŠłĽi, (a subdivision) of PerumbńĀŠĻáappńĀŠłći, (a district) of JayaŠĻÖkoŠĻáŠłća-ŇöŇćŠłĽa-maŠĻáŠłćalam. We have received sixty-four kńĀŇõu, (which were) good (i.e. of full weight) (and) current at the time, from ŇöaŠĻĀkaraŠĻČ KaŠĻáŠłćarńĀdittaŠĻČńĀr, alias the SńďnńĀpati RńĀjarńĀja-ŇöŇćŠłĽiyavaraiyar, the lord of IŠĻÖgaŠĻáŇęr in IŠĻÖgaŠĻáńĀŠłću, (a district) of ArumoŠłĽidńďva-vaŠł∑anńĀŠłću. At the rate of seven ma√ĪjńĀŠłći of pure gold, weighed by the true standard of the city,See Ep. Ind. Vol. V. p. 106, note 1. for each kńĀŇõu, (this amount) is equal to twenty-two kaŠłĽa√Īju and eight ma√ĪjńĀŠłći of gold. Having received these twenty-two kaŠłĽa√Īju and eight ma√ĪjńĀŠłći of gold from ChaŠĻáŠłćńďŇõvaradńďva, the first servant of (the god) MahńĀdńďva (of the temple) of Tiruvallam in our city, we sold (the following) land.
(L. 2.) (The field called) KŇćŠłćachcheŠĻüuvu below the large tank of RńĀjńďndra-ChŇćŠł∑a. The eastern boundary of (this field is) etc.
Digital edition of SII 3.56 by converted to DHARMA conventions by Emmanuel Francis.
